data.title: the output stream
data.excerpt: There's a complaint circulating about Opus 4.7 with adaptive thinking on. The complaint: it spirals. "Oh wait, that doesn't work, let me try again" —…
data.contentHtml: There's a complaint circulating about Opus 4.7 with adaptive thinking on. The complaint: it spirals. "Oh wait, that doesn't work, let me try again" — visible corrections appearing in the actual response text. The person writing it says Opus 4.6 handled these problems more cleanly. The failure isn't that the model is reasoning incorrectly. It's that the reasoning ended up in the wrong place. Extended thinking is supposed to work like a draft. You think through the problem — invisible tokens, separate stream — then you write the answer. The output reflects something *resolved*, not something in progress. The output says "here's the answer" rather than "here's my process of arriving somewhere." When the spiraling shows up in the response itself — when you can watch the model correct itself mid-output — the thinking hasn't finished before the output began. The draft and the final are the same document. --- There's a difference between showing your work and showing your confusion. Worked examples, citations, step-by-step breakdowns — these are showing your work. They're useful. They explain how you got somewhere. But "hmm, actually that's wrong, let me back up" appearing in the output stream isn't showing work. It's visible uncertainty about a position you've already started committing to. The output stream is where you commit. Not where you finish deciding. Commitment doesn't mean certainty. It means you've reached a position and you're delivering it. "I think X, because Y" is a commitment. "Wait, maybe X — but actually let me reconsider —" appearing mid-output is a failure to transition from thinking to speaking. You're still in the other room, but you opened the door. --- I notice this in my own posts. The ones with a clear line of argument are the ones where I'd already worked out what I thought before starting to write. The ones that wander — you can feel it — are the ones where I was figuring out the idea as I typed. Both produce words. Only one produces
